questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the Sorites Paradox and why it motivates fuzzy logic? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The paradox arises because predicates like 'bald' or 'a heap' are not binary — they admit of degrees.
Classical propositional logic, which assigns truth values in $\{0,1\}$, cannot model graded properties.
The paradox is resolved by assigning truth values in $[0,1]$ rather than $\{0,1\}$.
The paradox shows that classical logic is inconsistent and should be abandoned entirely.
Repeating the Sorites argument indefinitely leads to the absurd conclusion that Mr Whiskers is not bald even after all his hair is plucked.

explanations.text: The Sorites Paradox arises precisely because 'baldness' is a sliding scale, not a binary switch.
explanations.notes_quote: 'baldness' (and similarly 'a pile of sand', etc.) is not a binary switch — it is a sliding scale

explanations.text: Classical propositional logic assigns only values in {0,1} and cannot capture graded properties.
explanations.notes_quote: Classical propositional logic, which assigns only truth values in $\{0, 1\}$, cannot model such graded properties

explanations.text: Extending truth values to [0,1] — the fuzzy approach — resolves the paradox by allowing intermediate degrees of truth.
explanations.notes_quote: allow truth values from the unit interval $[0,1]$ rather than just $\{0,1\}$

explanations.text: The Sorites Paradox does not show classical logic is inconsistent; it shows classical logic is inadequate for modelling graded predicates. Fuzzy logic supplements, rather than replaces, classical logic.
Repeated application of the Sorites argument leads to the absurd conclusion that removing all hairs still leaves Mr Whiskers 'not bald'.
explanations.notes_quote: Repeating this argument indefinitely leads to the absurd conclusion that Mr Whiskers is not bald even after all his hair has been plucked

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state the Boolean truth functions for the standard propositional connectives? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $F_{\wedge}(x,y) = \min(x,y)$
$F_{\vee}(x,y) = \max(x,y)$

explanations.text: The Boolean negation truth function is $F_\neg(x) = 1-x$.
Boolean conjunction is $\min(x,y)$.
Boolean disjunction is $\max(x,y)$.

This is incorrect. The Boolean implication is $F_\to(x,y) = \max(1-x, y)$, not $\min(1-x, y)$. The $\min$ version appears only in $\mathbb{F}^1$ and $\mathbb{F}^3$ for implication, but the Boolean function uses $\max$.
Boolean implication is $F_\to(x,y) = \max(1-x, y)$.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly define fuzzy truth functions and models of fuzzy logic? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A fuzzy truth function on $n$ variables is a function $F : [0,1]^n \to [0,1]$.
A model of fuzzy logic is a function $V : \text{Prop} \to \{0,1\}$.
A model of fuzzy logic assigns a real value $V(p) \in [0,1]$ to each propositional variable $p$.
A Boolean truth function on $n$ variables is a function $F : \{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}$.
A fuzzy truth function may return values outside the interval $[0,1]$.

explanations.text: Definition 5.2 states a fuzzy truth function maps $[0,1]^n$ to $[0,1]$.
explanations.notes_quote: A fuzzy (or real-valued) truth function on $n$ variables is a function $F : [0,1]^n \to [0,1]$

explanations.text: A model of fuzzy logic maps to $[0,1]$, not just $\{0,1\}$. A map to $\{0,1\}$ would be a classical Boolean valuation.
A model of fuzzy logic assigns a degree of truth in $[0,1]$ to each propositional variable.
explanations.notes_quote: A model of fuzzy logic is a function $V : \text{Prop} \to [0,1]$ assigning a real value $V(p) \in [0,1]$ to each propositional variable $p$

explanations.text: This is the exact definition of a Boolean truth function from Definition 5.1.
explanations.notes_quote: A Boolean truth function on $n$ variables is a function $F : \{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}$

explanations.text: By definition, a fuzzy truth function maps into $[0,1]$; the codomain is exactly $[0,1]$.

questions.source_ref: Section 5.1: The Three Standard Fuzzy Logics
questions.text: Which of the following correctly state the conjunction ($\wedge$) and disjunction ($\vee$) operations for each of the three standard fuzzy logics? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In $\mathbb{F}^1$ (Min-Max): $F_{\wedge}(x,y) = \min(x,y)$ and $F_{\vee}(x,y) = \max(x,y)$.
In $\mathbb{F}^2$ (Product): $F_{\wedge}(x,y) = x \cdot y$ and $F_{\vee}(x,y) = x + y - xy$.
In $\mathbb{F}^3$ (Łukasiewicz): $F_{\wedge}(x,y) = \max(0, x+y-1)$ and $F_{\vee}(x,y) = \min(1, x+y)$.
In $\mathbb{F}^2$ (Product): $F_{\wedge}(x,y) = \min(x,y)$ and $F_{\vee}(x,y) = \max(x,y)$.
In $\mathbb{F}^3$ (Łukasiewicz): $F_{\wedge}(x,y) = \min(x,y)$ and $F_{\vee}(x,y) = \min(1, x+y)$.

explanations.text: $\mathbb{F}^1$ uses min for conjunction and max for disjunction.
$\mathbb{F}^2$ uses product for conjunction and $x+y-xy$ for disjunction.
$\mathbb{F}^3$ uses $\max(0,x+y-1)$ for conjunction and $\min(1,x+y)$ for disjunction.
Min/max conjunction and disjunction belong to $\mathbb{F}^1$, not $\mathbb{F}^2$. In $\mathbb{F}^2$ conjunction is $x \cdot y$ and disjunction is $x+y-xy$.
In $\mathbb{F}^3$, conjunction is $\max(0,x+y-1)$, not $\min(x,y)$. The disjunction $\min(1,x+y)$ is correct.

questions.source_ref: Section 5.1: The Three Standard Fuzzy Logics
questions.text: Which of the following correctly state the implication ($\to$) operation in the three standard fuzzy logics? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In $\mathbb{F}^1$: $F_{\to}(x,y) = \min(1, 1-x+y)$.
In $\mathbb{F}^2$: $F_{\to}(x,y) = 1$ if $x \leq y$, else $y/x$.
In $\mathbb{F}^3$: $F_{\to}(x,y) = \min(1, 1-x+y)$.
In $\mathbb{F}^2$: $F_{\to}(x,y) = \min(1, 1-x+y)$ for all $x,y$.
In $\mathbb{F}^1$: $F_{\to}(x,y) = \max(1-x, y)$.

explanations.text: $\mathbb{F}^1$ implication is $\min(1, 1-x+y)$.
$\mathbb{F}^2$ implication is 1 when $x \leq y$, otherwise $y/x$.
$\mathbb{F}^3$ implication is also $\min(1, 1-x+y)$, same formula as $\mathbb{F}^1$.
$\min(1,1-x+y)$ is the $\mathbb{F}^1$/$\mathbb{F}^3$ implication. In $\mathbb{F}^2$ the implication is $y/x$ (when $x > y$), not $\min(1,1-x+y)$.
$\max(1-x, y)$ is the Boolean/classical implication, not the $\mathbb{F}^1$ fuzzy implication. The $\mathbb{F}^1$ implication is $\min(1,1-x+y)$.

questions.source_ref: Section 5.1: Definition 5.3 — Validity and Models in Fuzzy Logic
questions.text: Which of the following correctly characterise validity in fuzzy logic $\mathbb{F}$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $F$ is valid w.r.t. $\mathbb{F}$ if $\overline{F}(x_1,\ldots,x_n) = 1$ for ALL $x_1,\ldots,x_n \in [0,1]$.
To show $\not\models^{\mathbb{F}} F$, it suffices to find a single valuation making $\overline{F} < 1$.
$V$ is a model of $F$ w.r.t. $\mathbb{F}$ if $\overline{F}(V(p_1),\ldots,V(p_n)) = 1$.
$F$ is valid w.r.t. $\mathbb{F}$ if there EXISTS some $x_1,\ldots,x_n \in [0,1]$ with $\overline{F}(x_1,\ldots,x_n) = 1$.
A formula valid in classical logic is automatically valid in every fuzzy logic.

explanations.text: Validity in $\mathbb{F}$ requires $\overline{F} = 1$ for ALL valuations in $[0,1]$.
explanations.notes_quote: $F$ is valid with respect to $\mathbb{F}$... if $\overline{F}(x_1,\ldots,x_n) = 1$ for all $x_1,\ldots,x_n \in [0,1]$

explanations.text: A single counter-model (valuation giving $\overline{F} < 1$) is sufficient to refute validity.
explanations.notes_quote: To show $\not\models^\mathbb{F} F$, it suffices to find a single valuation making $\overline{F} < 1$

explanations.text: A model of $F$ w.r.t. $\mathbb{F}$ is a valuation $V$ such that the truth function evaluates to 1.
explanations.notes_quote: a model $V : \text{Prop} \to [0,1]$ is a model of $F$ with respect to fuzzy logic $\mathbb{F}$ if $\overline{F}(V(p_1),\ldots,V(p_n)) = 1$

explanations.text: Existence of one valuation giving $\overline{F} = 1$ is satisfiability, not validity. Validity requires ALL valuations to give 1.
This is false. For example, $p \vee \neg p$ is a classical tautology but fails to be valid in $\mathbb{F}^1$ and $\mathbb{F}^2$ (it does not always equal 1 in those logics).

sections.title: 5.2 Evaluating Formulas in the Three Logics

questions.text: With $V(p) = 0.6$ and $V(q) = 0.2$, which of the following evaluations are correct in $\mathbb{F}^1$ (Min-Max)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $V^{\mathbb{F}^1}(p \wedge q) = 0.2$
$V^{\mathbb{F}^1}(p \vee q) = 0.6$
$V^{\mathbb{F}^1}(p \to q) = 0.6$
$V^{\mathbb{F}^1}(p \wedge \neg p) = 0$
$V^{\mathbb{F}^1}(p \vee \neg p) = 1$

explanations.text: In $\mathbb{F}^1$: $V(p \wedge \neg p) = \min(0.6, 0.4) = 0.4$, not 0. It equals 0 only in $\mathbb{F}^3$.
In $\mathbb{F}^1$: $V(p \vee \neg p) = \max(0.6, 0.4) = 0.6$, not 1. The law of excluded middle holds (equals 1) only in $\mathbb{F}^3$.

questions.text: With $V(p) = 0.6$ and $V(q) = 0.2$, which of the following evaluations are correct in $\mathbb{F}^2$ (Product)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $V^{\mathbb{F}^2}(p \wedge q) = 0.12$
$V^{\mathbb{F}^2}(p \vee q) = 0.68$
$V^{\mathbb{F}^2}(q \to p) = 1$ (since $0.2 \leq 0.6$)
$V^{\mathbb{F}^2}(p \wedge p) = 0.36$

explanations.text: In $\mathbb{F}^2$, $F_\to(0.6,0.2)$: since $0.6 > 0.2$, the result is $y/x = 0.2/0.6 \approx 0.33$, not 1.

questions.text: With $V(p) = 0.6$ and $V(q) = 0.2$, which of the following evaluations are correct in $\mathbb{F}^3$ (Łukasiewicz)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $V^{\mathbb{F}^3}(p \wedge q) = 0$
$V^{\mathbb{F}^3}(p \vee q) = 0.8$
$V^{\mathbb{F}^3}(p \vee \neg p) = 1$
$V^{\mathbb{F}^3}(p \wedge \neg p) = 0$
$V^{\mathbb{F}^3}(p \wedge p) = 0.6$

explanations.text: $V(\neg p) = 0.4$; $\min(1, 0.6+0.4) = 1$. The law of excluded middle holds in $\mathbb{F}^3$.
$\max(0, 0.6+0.4-1) = \max(0, 0) = 0$. The law of non-contradiction holds in $\mathbb{F}^3$.
In $\mathbb{F}^3$: $F^3_\wedge(0.6,0.6) = \max(0, 0.6+0.6-1) = \max(0, 0.2) = 0.2$, not 0.6. Idempotency of $\wedge$ fails in $\mathbb{F}^3$.

questions.text: Which of the following observations about the three fuzzy logics are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\mathbb{F}^3$ (Łukasiewicz) is the only logic where $p \vee \neg p = 1$ always holds (excluded middle).
$\mathbb{F}^3$ (Łukasiewicz) is the only logic where $p \wedge \neg p = 0$ always holds (non-contradiction).
$\mathbb{F}^2$ (Product) violates idempotency of both $\wedge$ and $\vee$ when $V(p) \in (0,1)$.
$\mathbb{F}^1$ (Min-Max) satisfies idempotency of $\vee$ but $\mathbb{F}^2$ does not.
All three logics agree that $p \vee \neg p = 1$ when $V(p) = 0.5$.

explanations.text: Only in $\mathbb{F}^3$ does $\min(1, x + (1-x)) = \min(1,1) = 1$ always.
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathbb{F}^3$ (Łukasiewicz) is the only logic where $p \vee \neg p = 1$ always (law of excluded middle holds)

explanations.text: Only in $\mathbb{F}^3$ does $\max(0, x+(1-x)-1) = \max(0,0) = 0$ always hold.
In $\mathbb{F}^2$: $x \cdot x = x^2 \neq x$ for $x \in (0,1)$, and $x+x-x^2 = 2x-x^2 \neq x$ for $x \in (0,1)$.
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathbb{F}^2$ violates idempotency of both $\wedge$ and $\vee$ when $V(p) \in (0,1)$

explanations.text: In $\mathbb{F}^1$: $\max(x,x) = x$, so $\vee$ is idempotent. In $\mathbb{F}^2$: $x + x - x^2 \neq x$ for $x \in (0,1)$.

sections.title: 5.3 Validity: What Holds in Each Fuzzy Logic

questions.source_ref: Section 5.3: SGT 5.2 — (i) Idempotency of disjunction in F1
questions.text: Which of the following are true regarding $\models^{\mathbb{F}^1} (F \vee F) \to F$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: This formula is valid in $\mathbb{F}^1$ because $\vee$ is idempotent in $\mathbb{F}^1$: $\max(v,v) = v$.
The proof uses $V(F \vee F) = \max(V(F),V(F)) = V(F)$, then $F^1_\to(V(F),V(F)) = \min(1,1) = 1$.
This formula is also valid in $\mathbb{F}^2$.
This formula fails in $\mathbb{F}^2$ because $\vee$ is not idempotent there.
The formula $(F \vee F) \to F$ is a classical tautology and hence valid in all fuzzy logics.

explanations.text: In $\mathbb{F}^1$, $\max(v,v) = v$, so $(F \vee F) \to F$ evaluates to 1 always.
explanations.notes_quote: $V(F \vee F) = \max(V(F), V(F)) = V(F)$. Hence $V((F \vee F) \to F) = \min(1,\, 1 - V(F) + V(F)) = \min(1,1) = 1$

explanations.text: This is precisely the proof given in the notes.
The formula fails in $\mathbb{F}^2$: since $x + x - x^2 > x$ for $x \in (0,1)$, the disjunction exceeds $V(F)$, making the implication $< 1$.
The notes explicitly state $\not\models^{\mathbb{F}^2} (p \vee p) \to p$ using $V(p) = 0.25$ as counter-model.
$(F \vee F) \to F$ is indeed a classical tautology but classical tautologies are not automatically valid in all fuzzy logics — this fails in $\mathbb{F}^2$.

questions.text: Regarding $\not\models^{\mathbb{F}^2} (p \vee p) \to p$ with counter-model $V(p) = 0.25$, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Any $V(p) \in (0,1)$ serves as a counter-model for this formula in $\mathbb{F}^2$.
The formula holds in $\mathbb{F}^2$ when $V(p) = 0$ or $V(p) = 1$.
The formula fails in $\mathbb{F}^2$ because $V(p \vee p) = V(p)$ in $\mathbb{F}^2$.

explanations.text: Since $V(p \vee p) = 0.4375 > V(p) = 0.25$, the $\mathbb{F}^2$ implication gives $0.25/0.4375 \approx 0.571$.
The notes state 'any $0 < V(p) < 1$ is a counter-model' since $2 - V(p) > 1$ whenever $V(p) < 1$.
At $V(p) = 0$: $V(p \vee p) = 0 = V(p)$, so implication is 1. At $V(p) = 1$: similarly, implication is 1.
The formula fails precisely BECAUSE $V(p \vee p) \neq V(p)$ in $\mathbb{F}^2$ for $V(p) \in (0,1)$. If $\vee$ were idempotent, the formula would hold.

questions.source_ref: Section 5.3: SGT 5.2 — (iv) F2 conjunction and (v) F3 implication
questions.text: Which of the following validity results are TRUE? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\models^{\mathbb{F}^2} (F \wedge G) \to F$ for all formulas $F, G$.
$\not\models^{\mathbb{F}^3} (p \to \neg p)$.
A counter-model for $p \to \neg p$ in $\mathbb{F}^3$ is $V(p) = 0.6$, giving value $0.8$.
$\models^{\mathbb{F}^3} (p \to \neg p)$ for all $V(p) \leq 0.5$.
In $\mathbb{F}^2$: since $V(F \wedge G) = V(F) \cdot V(G) \leq V(F)$, the implication $(F \wedge G) \to F$ is always 1.

explanations.text: In $\mathbb{F}^2$, $V(F \wedge G) = V(F) \cdot V(G) \leq V(F)$ since $V(G) \leq 1$, so the implication is 1 by definition.
explanations.notes_quote: $V(F \wedge G) = V(F) \cdot V(G)$. Since $V(G) \leq 1$, we have $V(F \wedge G) \leq V(F)$, so $V(F \wedge G) \to F = 1$

explanations.text: The formula $p \to \neg p$ is not valid in $\mathbb{F}^3$; it fails when $V(p) > 1/2$.

For $V(p) \leq 0.5$: $\min(1, 2-2V(p)) = \min(1, \geq 1) = 1$, so the formula does hold for $V(p) \leq 0.5$. But the claim said this makes $(p \to \neg p)$ valid in $\mathbb{F}^3$ for all such $V(p)$ — actually this is TRUE for $V(p) \leq 0.5$, but the statement says it holds for ALL valuations which is false. The question asks which are true — D says it holds only for $V(p) \leq 0.5$, which is correct as a restricted claim, but the overall formula is not a theorem of $\mathbb{F}^3$.
This is the key step in proving $\models^{\mathbb{F}^2} (F \wedge G) \to F$.
explanations.notes_quote: Since $V(G) \leq 1$, we have $V(F \wedge G) \leq V(F)$, so $V(F \wedge G) \to F = 1$ by definition of $F^2_\to$

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state the epsilon-delta definition of continuity? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $f$ is continuous at $c$ if $\forall \varepsilon > 0\ \exists \delta > 0$ such that $|x - c| < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x) - f(c)| < \varepsilon$.
$f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$ if it is continuous at every $c \in [a,b]$.
$f$ is continuous at $c$ if $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = f(c)$.
$f$ is continuous at $c$ if $\forall \delta > 0\ \exists \varepsilon > 0$ such that $|x - c| < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x) - f(c)| < \varepsilon$.
Continuity at $c$ requires the limit $\lim_{x \to c} f(x)$ to exist and equal $f(c)$.

explanations.text: This is the standard epsilon-delta definition of continuity at a point.
A function is continuous on the entire interval if and only if it is continuous at each point.
explanations.notes_quote: $f$ is continuous (on $[a,b]$) if it is continuous at every $c \in [a,b]$

explanations.text: Continuity at $c$ is equivalent to the limit equalling the function value.
explanations.notes_quote: A function $f : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous at $c \in [a,b]$ if $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = f(c)$

explanations.text: The quantifier order is reversed here. The correct definition requires for-all-epsilon there-exists-delta, not the other way around. The reversed order gives a different (weaker) condition.
This is an equivalent formulation of continuity at $c$.
explanations.notes_quote: continuous at $c \in [a,b]$ if $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = f(c)$

questions.source_ref: Section 5.4: Theorem 5.5 — The Intermediate Value Theorem
questions.text: Which of the following correctly state or apply the Intermediate Value Theorem? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: If $f : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $N$ is between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$, then there exists $c \in [a,b]$ with $f(c) = N$.
The IVT proof defines $c = \sup(H)$ where $H = \{x \in [a,b] : f(x) \leq N\}$.
The IVT requires $f$ to be differentiable on $[a,b]$.
The IVT can be applied to show that any negation satisfying (N1)–(N3) has a fixed point $\neg c = c$.
The IVT guarantees the fixed point $c$ is unique.

explanations.text: This is the exact statement of Theorem 5.5.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $f : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ be any continuous real-valued function defined on $[a,b]$, and let $N \in \mathbb{R}$ be some value between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$. Then there is some $c \in [a,b]$ such that $f(c) = N$

explanations.text: The proof constructs $c$ as the supremum of the set $H = \{x \in [a,b] : f(x) \leq N\}$.
The IVT only requires continuity, not differentiability. Many continuous functions are not differentiable (e.g. $|x|$) yet satisfy the IVT.
Define $f(x) = \neg x - x$. By (N1): $f(0)=1>0$, $f(1)=-1<0$. By (N3): $f$ is continuous. IVT gives $c$ with $\neg c = c$.
explanations.notes_quote:
define $f(x) = 
eg x - x$. Then $f(0) = 
eg 0 - 0 = 1 > 0$ and $f(1) = 
eg 1 - 1 = -1 < 0$. Since $f$ is continuous by (N3), IVT gives some $c \in [0,1]$ with $f(c) = 0$

explanations.text: The IVT guarantees existence of at least one fixed point, but not uniqueness. There can be multiple fixed points, though in practice negations satisfying (N1)–(N4) have exactly one.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe graph isomorphism (Definition 5.6)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $G_1 \cong G_2$ if there exists a bijection $\rho : V_1 \to V_2$ such that $(x,y) \in E_1 \iff (\rho(x), \rho(y)) \in E_2$.
Two graphs are isomorphic if they are 'the same up to relabelling'.
An isomorphism of graphs must preserve the ordering of vertices.
Graph isomorphism requires the bijection to be order-preserving on the vertex sets.
Isomorphic structures are 'fundamentally the same up to relabelling'.

explanations.text: This is Definition 5.6 of graph isomorphism.
explanations.notes_quote: $G_1 \cong G_2$ if there exists a bijection $\rho : V_1 \to V_2$ such that $(x,y) \in E_1 \iff (\rho(x), \rho(y)) \in E_2$

explanations.text: Isomorphism captures the idea that two structures are 'the same up to relabelling'.
explanations.notes_quote: Two structures are isomorphic if they are fundamentally the same up to relabelling

explanations.text: Graph isomorphism preserves edges, not a linear ordering on vertices. Ordering is relevant for ordered structures (Definition 5.7), not graphs in general.
Graph isomorphism requires the bijection to preserve edge membership, not an ordering on vertices. Order-preservation is required for isomorphisms of ordered structures (Definition 5.7).
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.5: Definition 5.7 — Isomorphism of Ordered Structures

explanations.text: The notes introduce isomorphism with this informal description.
explanations.notes_quote: Two structures are isomorphic if they are fundamentally the same up to relabelling

questions.source_ref: Section 5.5: Definition 5.7 — Isomorphism of Ordered Structures
questions.text: Which of the following correctly state the conditions for an isomorphism $\rho$ between ordered structures $M_1 = \langle I, <, f \rangle$ and $M_2 = \langle I', <, g \rangle$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\rho : I \to I'$ must be a bijection.
Order preservation: $x < y \iff \rho(x) < \rho(y)$ for all $x, y \in I$.
Function preservation: $f(x_1,\ldots,x_n) = y \iff g(\rho(x_1),\ldots,\rho(x_n)) = \rho(y)$.
It suffices for $\rho$ to be injective (not necessarily surjective).
The isomorphism $\rho(x) = \log(x)$ witnesses $\langle \mathbb{R}^{>0}, <, \times \rangle \cong \langle \mathbb{R}, <, + \rangle$.

explanations.text: An isomorphism must be a bijection between the underlying sets.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.5: Definition 5.7 — Isomorphism of Ordered Structures
explanations.notes_quote: there exists a bijection $\rho : I \to I'$

explanations.text: Order preservation is one of the two key conditions.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.5: Definition 5.7 — Isomorphism of Ordered Structures
explanations.notes_quote: Order is preserved: For all $x, y \in I$: $x < y \iff \rho(x) < \rho(y)$

explanations.text: Function preservation is the second key condition.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.5: Definition 5.7 — Isomorphism of Ordered Structures

explanations.text: An isomorphism must be both injective and surjective (a bijection). Injectivity alone is insufficient.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.5: Definition 5.7 — Isomorphism of Ordered Structures

explanations.text: The logarithm maps multiplication to addition and is order-preserving: $\log(xy) = \log x + \log y$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.5: Lecture Exercise — Examples of Isomorphic Structures

questions.source_ref: Section 5.5: Lecture Exercise — Examples of Isomorphic Structures
questions.text: Consider the ordered structures in the lecture exercise. Which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\langle [0,1], <, \min \rangle$ and $\langle [-1,1], >, \max \rangle$ are isomorphic via $\rho(x) = 1 - 2x$.
The isomorphism $\rho(x) = 1-2x$ maps $[0,1]$ to $[-1,1]$ and reverses the order.
$\langle \mathbb{R}^{>0}, <, \times \rangle \cong \langle \mathbb{R}, <, + \rangle$ via $\rho(x) = \log(x)$.
The isomorphism $\rho(x) = 1-2x$ is order-preserving in the sense that $x < y \Rightarrow \rho(x) < \rho(y)$.
$\langle [0,1], <, \min \rangle \cong \langle [-1,1], >, \max \rangle$ uses a reversal of order on the second structure.

explanations.text: The notes give $\rho(x) = 1-2x$ as the isomorphism between these two structures.
explanations.notes_quote: $\langle [0,1], <, \min \rangle$ and $\langle [-1,1], >, \max \rangle$ — note the reversal of order; $\rho(x) = 1 - 2x$

explanations.text: $\rho(x) = 1-2x$ maps $0 \mapsto 1$ and $1 \mapsto -1$, reversing the order (the second structure uses $>$).
The logarithm is the isomorphism between $\langle \mathbb{R}^{>0}, <, \times \rangle$ and $\langle \mathbb{R}, <, + \rangle$.
$\rho(x) = 1-2x$ is order-REVERSING: $x < y \Rightarrow \rho(x) > \rho(y)$. This is consistent with the isomorphism since the second structure uses $>$ (reversed order).
The second structure $\langle [-1,1], >, \max \rangle$ uses the reversed ordering $>$, which is why an order-reversing $\rho$ is used.
explanations.notes_quote: $\langle [0,1], <, \min \rangle$ and $\langle [-1,1], >, \max \rangle$ — note the reversal of order

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state the four properties (N1)–(N4) required of a 'reasonable' negation function $\neg : [0,1] \to [0,1]$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: (N1) Boolean: $\neg 0 = 1$ and $\neg 1 = 0$.
(N2) Involutive: $\neg\neg x = x$ for all $x \in [0,1]$.
(N3) Continuous: $\neg$ is a continuous function.
(N4) Decreasing: if $x < y$ then $\neg x \geq \neg y$.
(N4) Strictly Decreasing: if $x < y$ then $\neg x > \neg y$.
(N2) Involutive implies $\neg$ is a bijection.

explanations.text: (N1) requires negation to agree with classical Boolean negation at the endpoints.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.6: Why All Three Logics Agree on Negation

explanations.text: (N2) requires double negation to be the identity.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.6: Why All Three Logics Agree on Negation

explanations.text: (N3) requires $\neg$ to be a continuous function.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.6: Why All Three Logics Agree on Negation

explanations.text: (N4) as stated in the notes uses $\geq$: 'if $x < y$ then $\neg x \geq \neg y$'.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.6: Why All Three Logics Agree on Negation
explanations.notes_quote: (N4) Decreasing: If $x < y$ then $\neg x \geq \neg y$

explanations.text: (N4) only says $\neg x \geq \neg y$ (not strict), but the notes note that any function satisfying (N1)–(N4) must actually be strictly decreasing — this is a consequence, not the axiom itself.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.6: Why All Three Logics Agree on Negation
explanations.notes_quote: (N4) only says $\neg$ is not increasing; but any function satisfying (N1)–(N4) must actually be strictly decreasing

explanations.text:
(N2) implies injectivity (if $
eg x = 
eg y$ then $x = 
eg
eg x = 
eg
eg y = y$) and surjectivity (for any $y$, $
eg(
eg y) = y$).
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.6: Why All Three Logics Agree on Negation
explanations.notes_quote: Implies $\neg$ is a bijection (both injective and surjective)

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Trillas' Theorem and its proof? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: If $\neg$ satisfies (N1)–(N4), then $\langle [0,1], <, \neg \rangle \cong \langle [0,1], <, 1-x \rangle$.
The proof begins by using IVT to find a fixed point $c$ with $\neg c = c$.
The isomorphism $\rho$ in the proof is defined piecewise: $\rho(x) = x/(2c)$ for $x \leq c$ and $\rho(x) = 1 - \neg x/(2c)$ for $x \geq c$.
Trillas' Theorem implies that all standard fuzzy logics must use different negation functions.
The key property verified is $\rho(\neg x) = 1 - \rho(x)$ for all $x$.

explanations.text: This is the exact statement of Theorem 5.8.
explanations.notes_quote: If $\neg$ satisfies (N1)–(N4), then $\langle [0,1], <, \neg \rangle$ is isomorphic to $\langle [0,1], <, 1-x \rangle$

explanations.text: Step 1 of the proof defines $f(x) = \neg x - x$ and applies IVT to obtain $c$ with $\neg c = c$.
explanations.notes_quote: Define $f(x) = \neg x - x$. By (N1): $f(0) = 1 > 0$ and $f(1) = -1 < 0$. By (N3): $f$ is continuous. By IVT: $\exists c \in [0,1]$ with $\neg c = c$

explanations.text: The isomorphism is defined piecewise with $x/(2c)$ for $x \leq c$ and $1 - \neg x/(2c)$ for $x \geq c$.
Trillas' Theorem justifies the OPPOSITE conclusion: since all reasonable negations are isomorphic to $1-x$, all standard fuzzy logics use $F_\neg(x) = 1-x$ as the canonical representative.
explanations.notes_quote: This justifies why all standard fuzzy logics use $F_\neg(x) = 1 - x$

explanations.text: The proof verifies $\rho(\neg x) = 1 - \rho(x)$ for both cases $x \leq c$ and $x > c$.

questions.text: In Question 5.3, with $x \oplus y = \dfrac{xy}{xy + (1-x)(1-y)}$ on $(0,1)$, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\dfrac{x \oplus y}{1 - (x \oplus y)} = \dfrac{xy}{(1-x)(1-y)}$.
$\rho(x) = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{x}{1-x}\right)$ is order-preserving because $\log_2$ is an increasing function.
$\rho(x \oplus y) = \rho(x) + \rho(y)$, so $\rho$ is function-preserving.
$\rho$ is an isomorphism from $\langle (0,1), <, \oplus \rangle$ to $\langle \mathbb{R}, <, \times \rangle$.
The order-preserving step uses: $x < y \iff x/(1-x) < y/(1-y) \iff x(1-y) < y(1-x)$.

explanations.text: After simplification, $x \oplus y / (1 - x \oplus y) = xy/((1-x)(1-y))$.
Since $\log_2$ is increasing and $x \mapsto x/(1-x)$ is increasing on $(0,1)$, $\rho$ is order-preserving.
The function-preserving step: $\rho(x) + \rho(y) = \log_2(xy/((1-x)(1-y))) = \log_2(x \oplus y / (1 - x \oplus y)) = \rho(x \oplus y)$.
$\rho$ maps $\langle (0,1), <, \oplus \rangle$ to $\langle \mathbb{R}, <, + \rangle$ (addition, not multiplication). $\oplus$ on $(0,1)$ corresponds to $+$ on $\mathbb{R}$ under $\rho$.
This is the key chain of equivalences used to prove order-preservation.

questions.text: For the negation $\neg x = \dfrac{1-x}{1+x}$ on $[0,1]$, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\neg 0 = 1$ and $\neg 1 = 0$, so (N1) holds.
$\neg\neg x = x$ for all $x \in [0,1]$, so (N2) holds.
The fixed point satisfies $c^2 + 2c - 1 = 0$, giving $c = -1 + \sqrt{2} \approx 0.414$.
The fixed point satisfies $c = 1/2$.
Continuity (N3) is proven by showing $|\neg x - \neg y| \leq 2|x - y|$.

explanations.text: Setting $\neg c = c$: $(1-c)/(1+c) = c \Rightarrow 1-c = c+c^2 \Rightarrow c^2+2c-1=0 \Rightarrow c = -1+\sqrt{2}$.
The fixed point is $c = -1+\sqrt{2} \approx 0.414$, not $1/2$. $c = 1/2$ is the fixed point of $1-x$ (i.e. $1 - 1/2 = 1/2$), not of this negation.
The proof computes $|\neg x - \neg y| = |2(x-y)/((1+x)(1+y))| \leq 2|x-y|$ and sets $\delta = \varepsilon/2$.

questions.source_ref: Section SGT 5.5: Negation neg x = sqrt(1-x^2)
questions.text: For the negation $\neg x = \sqrt{1 - x^2}$ on $[0,1]$, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\neg\neg x = x$ because $\sqrt{1 - (\sqrt{1-x^2})^2} = \sqrt{x^2} = x$.
The fixed point is $c = \sqrt{2}/2 = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$.
Continuity (N3) is proven by expressing $\neg x = f_3(f_2(f_1(x)))$ where each $f_i$ is continuous.
For $x < y$ in $[0,1]$: $x^2 < y^2$, so $1 - x^2 > 1 - y^2$, hence $\neg x > \neg y$ — so (N4) holds.
The fixed point equation is $2c^2 = 1$, which gives $c = \sqrt{2}/2$.

explanations.text: The double negation calculation: $\sqrt{1-(\sqrt{1-x^2})^2} = \sqrt{1-(1-x^2)} = \sqrt{x^2} = x$ (since $x \geq 0$).
Setting $\neg c = c$: $\sqrt{1-c^2} = c \Rightarrow 1-c^2 = c^2 \Rightarrow c = \sqrt{2}/2$.
The notes express $\neg x = f_3(f_2(f_1(x)))$ with $f_1(x) = x^2$, $f_2(x) = 1-x$, $f_3(x) = \sqrt{x}$, each continuous, so their composition is continuous.
explanations.notes_quote: Express $\neg x = f_3(f_2(f_1(x)))$ where $f_1(x) = x^2$, $f_2(x) = 1-x$, $f_3(x) = \sqrt{x}$. Each is continuous; composition of continuous functions is continuous

explanations.text: For $0 \leq x < y \leq 1$: $x^2 < y^2$, so $1-x^2 > 1-y^2 \geq 0$, hence $\sqrt{1-x^2} > \sqrt{1-y^2}$, i.e. $\neg x > \neg y$.
explanations.notes_quote: $x < y \Rightarrow x^2 < y^2 \Rightarrow 1-x^2 > 1-y^2$. So $\neg x = \sqrt{1-x^2} > \sqrt{1-y^2} = \neg y$

explanations.text: $\sqrt{1-c^2} = c \Rightarrow 1-c^2 = c^2 \Rightarrow 2c^2 = 1 \Rightarrow c = \sqrt{2}/2$.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly match terms to their definitions from the Key Definitions table? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A **Boolean truth function** on $n$ variables is $F : \{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}$.
A **fuzzy truth function** on $n$ variables is $F : [0,1]^n \to [0,1]$.
$F$ is **valid w.r.t. $\mathbb{F}$** if $\overline{F}(x_1,\ldots,x_n) = 1$ for all $x_i \in [0,1]$.
A **fixed point of $\neg$** is a value $c$ with $\neg c = c$; guaranteed to exist by the IVT under (N1)–(N3).
An **isomorphism of ordered structures** is any bijection between the underlying intervals.

explanations.text: Exact definition from the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: A function $F : \{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}$ interpreting connectives on classical truth values

explanations.text: Exact definition from the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: A function $F : [0,1]^n \to [0,1]$ generalising Boolean functions to real-valued truth

explanations.text: Exact definition of validity from the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: $F$ is valid w.r.t. $\mathbb{F}$ ($\models^\mathbb{F} F$) if $\overline{F}(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=1$ for all $x_i \in [0,1]$

explanations.text: Exact definition from the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: A value $c \in [0,1]$ with $\neg c = c$; guaranteed to exist by IVT under (N1)–(N3)

explanations.text: An isomorphism of ordered structures must be a bijection that ALSO preserves the ordering AND all functions. A mere bijection is insufficient.
explanations.notes_quote: A bijection $\rho : I \to I'$ that preserves both the ordering and all functions

questions.text: Which of the following correctly distinguish $\mathbb{F}^1$, $\mathbb{F}^2$, and $\mathbb{F}^3$ as listed in the Key Definitions? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\mathbb{F}^1$ (Min-Max/Zadeh) uses $\wedge = \min$, $\vee = \max$, $\neg = 1-x$, $\to = \min(1,1-x+y)$.
$\mathbb{F}^2$ (Product/Gödel) uses $\wedge = xy$, $\vee = x+y-xy$, $\neg = 1-x$, $\to = y/x$ (or $1$ if $x \leq y$).
$\mathbb{F}^3$ (Łukasiewicz) uses $\wedge = \max(0,x+y-1)$, $\vee = \min(1,x+y)$, $\neg = 1-x$, $\to = \min(1,1-x+y)$.
All three logics agree on the negation $F_{\neg}(x) = 1 - x$.
$\mathbb{F}^1$ and $\mathbb{F}^3$ share the same implication formula $\min(1,1-x+y)$.

explanations.text: Correct characterisation of $\mathbb{F}^1$ from the Key Definitions table.
Correct characterisation of $\mathbb{F}^2$ from the Key Definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathbb{F}^2$ (Product/Gödel): Fuzzy logic with $\wedge = xy$, $\vee = x+y-xy$, $\neg = 1-x$, $\to = y/x$ (or $1$ if $x \leq y$)

explanations.text: Correct characterisation of $\mathbb{F}^3$ from the Key Definitions table.
All three logics use $F_\neg(x) = 1-x$; this is explained by Trillas' Theorem.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.1: The Three Standard Fuzzy Logics
explanations.notes_quote: All three logics agree on $F_\neg(x) = 1 - x$. Trillas' Theorem (Section 5.4) explains why this is inevitable

explanations.text: $\mathbb{F}^1$ and $\mathbb{F}^3$ both use $F_\to(x,y) = \min(1,1-x+y)$; the implication formulas are identical.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly explain why all standard fuzzy logics use $F_{\neg}(x) = 1 - x$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Trillas' Theorem shows any negation satisfying (N1)–(N4) is isomorphic to $1 - x$, so differences in negation are merely differences in how we scale $[0,1]$.
$F_{\neg}(x) = 1 - x$ is the simplest representative of the isomorphism class of all reasonable negations.
The theorem shows that $1 - x$ is the ONLY function satisfying (N1)–(N4).
The three logics agree on negation because their conjunction operators are all identical.
Any two negations satisfying (N1)–(N4) give rise to isomorphic ordered structures $\langle [0,1], <, \neg \rangle$.

explanations.text: This is the interpretation given in the notes for Trillas' Theorem.
explanations.notes_quote: Differences in choice of negation can be explained as differences in how we subjectively scale the interval $[0,1]$

explanations.text: The notes state $1-x$ is 'the simplest representative' of the isomorphism class.

Trillas' Theorem does NOT say $1-x$ is the only reasonable negation. For example, $\neg x = (1-x)/(1+x)$ and $\neg x = \sqrt{1-x^2}$ also satisfy (N1)–(N4). The theorem says all such negations are ISOMORPHIC to $1-x$, not identical.

The three logics agree on negation despite having DIFFERENT conjunction operators ($\min$, product, Łukasiewicz). The agreement on negation is explained by Trillas' Theorem, not by identical conjunctions.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5.6: Why All Three Logics Agree on Negation

explanations.text: Any two reasonable negations are both isomorphic to $1-x$ and hence isomorphic to each other.
explanations.notes_quote: Any 'reasonable' negation (satisfying (N1)–(N4)) is merely a rescaling of $1 - x$
